Chapter 1: Scalar Conservation Laws and Shock Waves

CORE-014

1 Chapter 1: Scalar Conservation Laws and Shock Waves

The study of nonlinear partial differential equations begins naturally with scalar conservation laws, which model the fundamental physical principle that certain quantities are preserved in time. These equations arise throughout physics and engineering—from gas dynamics to traffic flow—and their analysis reveals phenomena absent in linear theory: finite-time blow-up of gradients, formation of discontinuous solutions, and the need for new mathematical frameworks to select physically meaningful solutions.

1.1 1.1 The Burgers Equation and Method of Characteristics

The prototype for scalar conservation laws is Burgers’ equation:

\[u_t + u u_x = 0\]

where \(u(x,t)\) represents the velocity field. This equation combines the simplest nonlinearity with conservation structure and exhibits all the essential phenomena of more complex systems.

Definition 1.1 (Scalar Conservation Law). A scalar conservation law is a first-order partial differential equation of the form \[u_t + f(u)_x = 0\] where \(u: \mathbb{R} \times [0,\infty) \to \mathbb{R}\) is the conserved quantity and \(f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is the flux function.

For Burgers’ equation, \(f(u) = u^2/2\), so \(f'(u) = u\). The method of characteristics transforms this PDE into a system of ODEs along characteristic curves.

Definition 1.2 (Characteristic Curves). For the conservation law \(u_t + f(u)_x = 0\), the characteristic curves are solutions to the ODE system: \[\frac{dx}{dt} = f'(u), \quad \frac{du}{dt} = 0\]

For Burgers’ equation, characteristics satisfy \(dx/dt = u\) and \(du/dt = 0\). Thus \(u\) is constant along characteristics, and each characteristic is a straight line with slope \(u(x_0, 0)\) where \(x_0\) is the initial position.

Theorem 1.1 (Method of Characteristics Solution). Consider the initial value problem for Burgers’ equation: \[u_t + u u_x = 0, \quad u(x,0) = u_0(x)\] If \(u_0\) is smooth, then the solution is implicitly defined by: \[u(x,t) = u_0(\xi), \quad x = \xi + t u_0(\xi)\] where \(\xi\) is determined by solving the second equation for given \((x,t)\).

Proof. Along the characteristic through \((x_0, 0)\), we have \(x(t) = x_0 + t u_0(x_0)\) and \(u(x(t), t) = u_0(x_0)\). Setting \(\xi = x_0\) gives the implicit form. Differentiating the relations with respect to \(t\) and \(x\) verifies that \(u_t + u u_x = 0\). □

1.1.1 Example 1.1: Smooth Solution

Consider the initial condition \(u_0(x) = \sin(x)\). The characteristic equations give: \[x = \xi + t \sin(\xi), \quad u = \sin(\xi)\]

For small times, this defines a smooth solution. However, characteristics emanating from different initial points may intersect, leading to multivalued solutions.

1.2 1.2 Gradient Catastrophe and Shock Formation

The nonlinear nature of conservation laws leads to gradient blow-up in finite time, even for smooth initial data.

Definition 1.3 (Gradient Catastrophe). A gradient catastrophe occurs when \(u_x \to -\infty\) at some finite time \(t^* > 0\), indicating that the method of characteristics breaks down.

Theorem 1.2 (Gradient Blow-up Time). For Burgers’ equation with smooth initial data \(u_0(x)\), if \(\min_x u_0'(x) < 0\), then the gradient catastrophe time is: \[t^* = -\frac{1}{\min_x u_0'(x)}\]

Proof. From the characteristic solution, we compute: \[u_x = \frac{u_0'(\xi)}{1 + t u_0'(\xi)}\] The denominator vanishes when \(t = -1/u_0'(\xi)\), and the minimum occurs at the point where \(u_0'(\xi)\) is most negative. □

After the gradient catastrophe, characteristics cross and the classical solution becomes multivalued. This signals the formation of a shock wave—a discontinuity that propagates with finite speed.

1.2.1 Example 1.2: Shock Formation in Burgers’ Equation

Consider the Riemann problem with initial data: \[u_0(x) = \begin{cases} u_L & \text{if } x < 0 \\ u_R & \text{if } x > 0 \end{cases}\]

If \(u_L > u_R\), characteristics with slope \(u_L\) from the left intersect those with slope \(u_R\) from the right at time \(t^* = 0^+\). The resulting shock moves with speed \(s = (u_L + u_R)/2\).

using Plots, DifferentialEquations

function burgers_characteristics(u0, x_range, t_max, n_chars=50)
    x0_vals = range(x_range[1], x_range[2], length=n_chars)
    
    # For each initial position, compute characteristic
    characteristics = []
    for x0 in x0_vals
        u_val = u0(x0)
        t_vals = range(0, t_max, length=100)
        x_vals = x0 .+ t_vals * u_val
        push!(characteristics, (x_vals, t_vals, u_val))
    end
    
    return characteristics
end

# Example: u0(x) = -tanh(x) (smooth transition leading to shock)
u0(x) = -tanh(x)

chars = burgers_characteristics(u0, (-3, 3), 1.5, 30)

# Plot characteristics
p = plot(xlabel="x", ylabel="t", title="Burgers Equation Characteristics")
for (x_vals, t_vals, u_val) in chars
    plot!(x_vals, t_vals, color=:blue, alpha=0.3, legend=false)
end

# Mark shock formation region
plot!(p, [-1, 1], [1, 1], color=:red, linewidth=3, 
      label="Shock formation")

display(p)

1.3 1.3 Weak Solutions and Non-uniqueness

When classical solutions cease to exist, we must extend our notion of solution to include discontinuous functions.

Definition 1.4 (Weak Solution). A function \(u \in L^\infty(\mathbb{R} \times [0,\infty))\) is a weak solution of \(u_t + f(u)_x = 0\) if for all test functions \(\varphi \in C_0^\infty(\mathbb{R} \times [0,\infty))\): \[\int_0^\infty \int_\mathbb{R} (u \varphi_t + f(u) \varphi_x) \, dx \, dt + \int_\mathbb{R} u_0(x) \varphi(x,0) \, dx = 0\]

This definition makes sense even when \(u\) has discontinuities, since it only requires integration against smooth test functions.

Theorem 1.3 (Rankine-Hugoniot Jump Condition). If a weak solution \(u\) has a discontinuity along a curve \(x = X(t)\) with left and right limits \(u_L = u(X(t)^-, t)\) and \(u_R = u(X(t)^+, t)\), then the shock speed satisfies: \[\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{f(u_L) - f(u_R)}{u_L - u_R}\]

Proof. Apply the weak formulation to a test function concentrated near the shock. The jump in flux must balance the speed of the discontinuity. □

For Burgers’ equation with \(f(u) = u^2/2\), the Rankine-Hugoniot condition gives shock speed \(s = (u_L + u_R)/2\).

However, weak solutions are not unique. Given any shock solution, one can construct infinitely many other weak solutions by inserting “expansion shocks” where the solution jumps from low to high values.

1.3.1 Example 1.3: Non-uniqueness of Weak Solutions

Consider the Riemann problem for Burgers’ equation with \(u_L = 0\), \(u_R = 1\). Three weak solutions exist:

  1. Shock solution: \(u(x,t) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x < t/2 \\ 1 & \text{if } x > t/2 \end{cases}\)

  2. Rarefaction solution: \(u(x,t) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x < 0 \\ x/t & \text{if } 0 < x < t \\ 1 & \text{if } x > t \end{cases}\)

  3. Non-physical solutions: Various combinations of shocks and rarefactions.

1.4 1.4 Entropy Conditions and Admissibility

To select the physically correct solution among multiple weak solutions, we need additional admissibility criteria based on the second law of thermodynamics.

Definition 1.5 (Entropy Condition - Lax). A shock connecting states \(u_L\) and \(u_R\) is admissible if the characteristic speeds satisfy: \[f'(u_L) > s > f'(u_R)\] where \(s\) is the shock speed given by Rankine-Hugoniot.

For Burgers’ equation, this becomes \(u_L > s > u_R\), or equivalently \(u_L > u_R\). This eliminates expansion shocks where \(u_L < u_R\).

Definition 1.6 (Entropy Condition - Oleinik). For any \(u_1, u_2\) in the interval between \(u_L\) and \(u_R\): \[\frac{f(u_L) - f(u_1)}{u_L - u_1} \geq s \geq \frac{f(u_2) - f(u_R)}{u_2 - u_R}\]

This is equivalent to the Lax condition for scalar equations but generalizes to systems.

Definition 1.7 (Kruzkov Entropy Condition). A weak solution \(u\) satisfies the Kruzkov entropy condition if for all constants \(k \in \mathbb{R}\) and all non-negative test functions \(\varphi\): \[\int_0^\infty \int_\mathbb{R} (|u-k| \varphi_t + \text{sgn}(u-k)(f(u)-f(k)) \varphi_x) \, dx \, dt \geq 0\]

Theorem 1.4 (Kruzkov Uniqueness). The Kruzkov entropy condition selects a unique weak solution for scalar conservation laws with \(L^\infty\) initial data.

1.5 1.5 The Riemann Problem

The Riemann problem—solving the conservation law with piecewise constant initial data—is fundamental to understanding wave interactions.

Definition 1.8 (Riemann Problem). The Riemann problem for \(u_t + f(u)_x = 0\) consists of the initial value problem: \[u(x,0) = \begin{cases} u_L & \text{if } x < 0 \\ u_R & \text{if } x > 0 \end{cases}\]

For scalar equations, the solution depends on whether \(f\) is convex or concave in the interval \([u_L, u_R]\).

Theorem 1.5 (Riemann Problem Solution). For a convex flux \(f\) (i.e., \(f'' > 0\)): - If \(u_L > u_R\): The solution is a shock wave moving at speed \(s = \frac{f(u_L) - f(u_R)}{u_L - u_R}\). - If \(u_L < u_R\): The solution is a rarefaction wave: \(u(x,t) = (f')^{-1}(x/t)\) for \(f'(u_L) < x/t < f'(u_R)\).

1.5.1 Example 1.4: Rarefaction Waves

For Burgers’ equation with \(u_L = 0\), \(u_R = 1\), the rarefaction solution is: \[u(x,t) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x/t \leq 0 \\ x/t & \text{if } 0 < x/t < 1 \\ 1 & \text{if } x/t \geq 1 \end{cases}\]

This represents a smooth transition between the two constant states, spreading linearly in time.

function riemann_solution(u_L, u_R, x, t)
    """Exact Riemann solution for Burgers equation"""
    if t ≈ 0
        return x < 0 ? u_L : u_R
    end
    
    xi = x / t  # similarity variable
    
    if u_L > u_R
        # Shock wave
        s = (u_L + u_R) / 2  # shock speed
        return xi < s ? u_L : u_R
    else
        # Rarefaction wave
        if xi <= u_L
            return u_L
        elseif xi >= u_R
            return u_R
        else
            return xi  # u = x/t in rarefaction fan
        end
    end
end

# Plot Riemann solutions
x_vals = range(-2, 3, length=400)
times = [0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0]

p1 = plot(title="Shock Wave (u_L=1, u_R=0)", xlabel="x", ylabel="u")
for t in times
    u_vals = [riemann_solution(1.0, 0.0, x, t) for x in x_vals]
    plot!(x_vals, u_vals, label="t=$t", linewidth=2)
end

p2 = plot(title="Rarefaction Wave (u_L=0, u_R=1)", xlabel="x", ylabel="u")
for t in times
    u_vals = [riemann_solution(0.0, 1.0, x, t) for x in x_vals]
    plot!(x_vals, u_vals, label="t=$t", linewidth=2)
end

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(800,300))

1.6 1.6 Traffic Flow as a Conservation Law

A compelling application of scalar conservation laws is traffic flow modeling, where the conserved quantity is the density of vehicles.

Definition 1.9 (LWR Traffic Model). The Lighthill-Whitham-Richards model describes traffic density \(\rho(x,t)\) by: \[\rho_t + (\rho v(\rho))_x = 0\] where \(v(\rho)\) is the velocity-density relationship.

A common choice is \(v(\rho) = v_{\max}(1 - \rho/\rho_{\max})\), giving flux \(f(\rho) = v_{\max} \rho (1 - \rho/\rho_{\max})\).

This flux function is concave with maximum at \(\rho = \rho_{\max}/2\). Traffic jams correspond to shocks, while traffic flow resumption corresponds to rarefaction waves.

1.6.1 Example 1.5: Traffic Light Problem

Consider a traffic light at \(x = 0\) that turns green at \(t = 0\). The initial condition is: \[\rho(x,0) = \begin{cases} \rho_{\max} & \text{if } x < 0 \text{ (jammed traffic)} \\ 0 & \text{if } x > 0 \text{ (empty road)} \end{cases}\]

Since \(\rho_L = \rho_{\max} > \rho_R = 0\) and the flux is concave, this produces a rarefaction wave. Traffic density gradually decreases from maximum to zero as vehicles accelerate away from the light.

function traffic_flux(rho, rho_max=1.0, v_max=1.0)
    """Traffic flux function f(rho) = v_max * rho * (1 - rho/rho_max)"""
    return v_max * rho * (1 - rho / rho_max)
end

function traffic_velocity(rho, rho_max=1.0, v_max=1.0)
    """Traffic velocity function v(rho) = v_max * (1 - rho/rho_max)"""
    return v_max * (1 - rho / rho_max)
end

function traffic_riemann_solution(rho_L, rho_R, x, t, rho_max=1.0, v_max=1.0)
    """Riemann solution for LWR traffic model"""
    if t ≈ 0
        return x < 0 ? rho_L : rho_R
    end
    
    # Compute characteristic speeds
    c_L = traffic_velocity(rho_L, rho_max, v_max) - 
          v_max * rho_L / rho_max
    c_R = traffic_velocity(rho_R, rho_max, v_max) - 
          v_max * rho_R / rho_max
    
    xi = x / t
    
    if rho_L > rho_R && c_L > c_R
        # Shock (traffic jam formation)
        f_L = traffic_flux(rho_L, rho_max, v_max)
        f_R = traffic_flux(rho_R, rho_max, v_max)
        s = (f_L - f_R) / (rho_L - rho_R)
        return xi < s ? rho_L : rho_R
    else
        # Rarefaction (traffic jam dissolution)
        if xi <= c_L
            return rho_L
        elseif xi >= c_R
            return rho_R
        else
            # Solve v(rho) - rho * v'(rho) = xi for rho
            # For our model: v_max(1 - rho/rho_max) - rho*(-v_max/rho_max) = xi
            # v_max(1 - rho/rho_max + rho/rho_max) = v_max = xi (this is wrong)
            # Correct: v_max(1 - 2*rho/rho_max) = xi
            rho = rho_max * (1 - xi/v_max) / 2
            return max(0, min(rho_max, rho))
        end
    end
end

# Simulate traffic light scenario
x_vals = range(-2, 3, length=400)
times = [0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0]

p = plot(title="Traffic Light: Green at t=0", 
         xlabel="Position x", ylabel="Traffic Density ρ")

for t in times
    rho_vals = [traffic_riemann_solution(1.0, 0.0, x, t) for x in x_vals]
    plot!(x_vals, rho_vals, label="t=$t", linewidth=2)
end

# Mark traffic light position
vline!([0], linestyle=:dash, color=:black, label="Traffic Light")

1.7 1.7 Numerical Methods

Numerical solution of conservation laws requires special care to capture shocks accurately while maintaining stability and conservation.

1.7.1 Lax-Friedrichs Scheme

The Lax-Friedrichs scheme replaces spatial derivatives with centered differences and temporal derivatives with forward differences, but uses a modified averaging:

\[u_i^{n+1} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i+1}^n + u_{i-1}^n) - \frac{\Delta t}{2\Delta x}(f(u_{i+1}^n) - f(u_{i-1}^n))\]

Theorem 1.6 (Lax-Friedrichs Stability). The Lax-Friedrichs scheme is stable under the CFL condition: \[\frac{\Delta t}{\Delta x} \max_u |f'(u)| \leq 1\]

1.7.2 Godunov Scheme

The Godunov scheme uses exact Riemann solutions to compute fluxes at cell interfaces:

\[u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - \frac{\Delta t}{\Delta x}(F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2})\]

where \(F_{i+1/2}\) is the flux computed from the Riemann problem between \(u_i^n\) and \(u_{i+1}^n\).

using LinearAlgebra

function lax_friedrichs_step(u, f, dx, dt)
    """Single time step of Lax-Friedrichs scheme"""
    n = length(u)
    u_new = zeros(n)
    
    # Periodic boundary conditions
    for i in 2:n-1
        u_new[i] = 0.5 * (u[i+1] + u[i-1]) - 
                   (dt/(2*dx)) * (f(u[i+1]) - f(u[i-1]))
    end
    
    # Handle boundaries with periodic conditions
    u_new[1] = 0.5 * (u[2] + u[n]) - (dt/(2*dx)) * (f(u[2]) - f(u[n]))
    u_new[n] = 0.5 * (u[1] + u[n-1]) - (dt/(2*dx)) * (f(u[1]) - f(u[n-1]))
    
    return u_new
end

function godunov_flux(u_L, u_R, f)
    """Godunov flux for Burgers equation"""
    if u_L > u_R
        # Shock
        if 0 <= (u_L + u_R)/2  # shock moves right
            return f(u_L)
        else  # shock moves left
            return f(u_R)
        end
    else
        # Rarefaction
        if u_L >= 0
            return f(u_L)
        elseif u_R <= 0
            return f(u_R)
        else  # 0 is in the rarefaction fan
            return f(0.0)
        end
    end
end

function godunov_step(u, f, dx, dt)
    """Single time step of Godunov scheme"""
    n = length(u)
    u_new = copy(u)
    
    for i in 2:n-1
        flux_left = godunov_flux(u[i-1], u[i], f)
        flux_right = godunov_flux(u[i], u[i+1], f)
        u_new[i] = u[i] - (dt/dx) * (flux_right - flux_left)
    end
    
    return u_new
end

# Test both schemes on Burgers equation
f_burgers(u) = 0.5 * u^2

# Initial condition: shock formation
x = range(-2, 2, length=100)
dx = x[2] - x[1]
u0 = [sin(π*xi) for xi in x]

# Time stepping parameters
dt = 0.4 * dx  # CFL condition
n_steps = 50

# Lax-Friedrichs evolution
u_lf = copy(u0)
for step in 1:n_steps
    u_lf = lax_friedrichs_step(u_lf, f_burgers, dx, dt)
end

# Godunov evolution  
u_god = copy(u0)
for step in 1:n_steps
    u_god = godunov_step(u_god, f_burgers, dx, dt)
end

# Plot comparison
p = plot(x, u0, label="Initial", linewidth=2, 
         title="Burgers Equation: Numerical Methods Comparison")
plot!(x, u_lf, label="Lax-Friedrichs", linewidth=2)
plot!(x, u_god, label="Godunov", linewidth=2)
xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("u")

1.8 1.8 High-Resolution Methods

Modern numerical methods achieve higher accuracy while maintaining sharp shock resolution through slope limiters and flux limiters.

Definition 1.10 (TVD Property). A numerical scheme is Total Variation Diminishing (TVD) if: \[TV(u^{n+1}) \leq TV(u^n)\] where \(TV(u) = \sum_i |u_{i+1} - u_i|\) is the total variation.

TVD schemes prevent spurious oscillations near shocks while maintaining high accuracy in smooth regions.

The MUSCL-Hancock scheme combines higher-order reconstruction with slope limiting:

  1. Reconstruct piecewise linear data in each cell
  2. Apply slope limiter to prevent oscillations
  3. Evolve using a two-step predictor-corrector method
function minmod(a, b)
    """Minmod slope limiter"""
    if a * b > 0
        return sign(a) * min(abs(a), abs(b))
    else
        return 0.0
    end
end

function muscl_reconstruction(u)
    """MUSCL reconstruction with minmod limiter"""
    n = length(u)
    u_left = zeros(n)
    u_right = zeros(n)
    
    for i in 2:n-1
        slope = minmod(u[i] - u[i-1], u[i+1] - u[i])
        u_left[i] = u[i] - 0.5 * slope
        u_right[i] = u[i] + 0.5 * slope
    end
    
    # Boundary conditions (copy)
    u_left[1] = u[1]
    u_right[1] = u[1]
    u_left[n] = u[n]
    u_right[n] = u[n]
    
    return u_left, u_right
end

function muscl_step(u, f, dx, dt)
    """MUSCL-Hancock scheme step"""
    u_left, u_right = muscl_reconstruction(u)
    n = length(u)
    u_new = copy(u)
    
    for i in 2:n-1
        # Compute fluxes at interfaces using Godunov method
        flux_left = godunov_flux(u_right[i-1], u_left[i], f)
        flux_right = godunov_flux(u_right[i], u_left[i+1], f)
        
        u_new[i] = u[i] - (dt/dx) * (flux_right - flux_left)
    end
    
    return u_new
end

# Compare with high-resolution method
u_muscl = copy(u0)
for step in 1:n_steps
    u_muscl = muscl_step(u_muscl, f_burgers, dx, dt)
end

# Enhanced comparison plot
p = plot(x, u0, label="Initial", linewidth=2, linestyle=:dash,
         title="High-Resolution Methods for Burgers Equation")
plot!(x, u_lf, label="Lax-Friedrichs", linewidth=2, alpha=0.7)
plot!(x, u_god, label="Godunov", linewidth=2, alpha=0.7) 
plot!(x, u_muscl, label="MUSCL", linewidth=3)
xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("u")

1.9 Exercises

  1. Characteristic Analysis: For the conservation law \(u_t + (u^3)_x = 0\) with initial data \(u_0(x) = e^{-x^2}\), find the time at which the first shock forms. Determine the location where this occurs.

  2. Entropy Production: Verify that the Kruzkov entropy condition is satisfied for the shock solution of Burgers’ equation with \(u_L = 1\), \(u_R = 0\). Show that it is violated for the expansion shock with \(u_L = 0\), \(u_R = 1\).

  3. Traffic Flow Simulation: Implement a Julia function to simulate the LWR traffic model with flux \(f(\rho) = \rho(1-\rho)\). Model a traffic light cycle: red for \(t \in [0,1]\), green for \(t \in [1,2]\), repeating. Use initial condition \(\rho(x,0) = 0.8\) for \(x < 0\) and \(\rho(x,0) = 0.2\) for \(x > 0\).

  4. Riemann Invariants: For the nonconvex flux \(f(u) = u(1-u)(u-a)\) with \(0 < a < 1\), analyze the Riemann problem for all possible combinations of left and right states. Identify regions where the solution contains shocks, rarefactions, or compound waves.

  5. Numerical Convergence: Implement the Lax-Wendroff scheme: \[u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - \frac{\Delta t}{2\Delta x}(f(u_{i+1}^n) - f(u_{i-1}^n)) + \frac{\Delta t^2}{2\Delta x^2}(a_{i+1/2}(f(u_{i+1}^n) - f(u_i^n)) - a_{i-1/2}(f(u_i^n) - f(u_{i-1}^n)))\] where \(a_{i+1/2} = f'((u_i^n + u_{i+1}^n)/2)\). Compare its accuracy with Godunov’s method for smooth and discontinuous solutions.

  6. Weakly Nonlinear Analysis: For small amplitude solutions to Burgers’ equation, derive the asymptotic expansion \(u = \epsilon u_1 + \epsilon^2 u_2 + O(\epsilon^3)\). Show that \(u_1\) satisfies the linear advection equation and \(u_2\) contains the first nonlinear corrections.

  7. Shock Fitting: Develop a shock-fitting algorithm that tracks shock positions explicitly rather than capturing them on a fixed grid. Apply this to the Riemann problem for Burgers’ equation and compare accuracy with shock-capturing methods.

  8. Conservation Properties: Prove that the Godunov scheme exactly conserves the total integral \(\int u(x,t) dx\) for any conservation law. Show that Lax-Friedrichs has a conservation error that decreases with grid refinement.

  9. Viscous Regularization: Study the vanishing viscosity limit for \(u_t + u u_x = \epsilon u_{xx}\) with \(\epsilon \to 0\). Use Julia to solve numerically and verify convergence to the entropy solution of Burgers’ equation.

  10. Multidimensional Extension: Extend the Lax-Friedrichs scheme to the two-dimensional Burgers’ equation \(u_t + u u_x + u u_y = 0\). Implement the method and test on radially symmetric initial data that leads to shock formation.

1.10 References

Evans, L. C. (2010). Partial Differential Equations. American Mathematical Society.

Godlewski, E., & Raviart, P.-A. (1996). Numerical Approximation of Hyperbolic Systems of Conservation Laws. Springer-Verlag.

Holden, H., & Risebro, N. H. (2015). Front Tracking for Hyperbolic Conservation Laws. Springer.

Kruzkov, S. N. (1970

2 Chapter 2: Systems of Conservation Laws

The study of systems of conservation laws represents one of the most challenging and richly structured areas of nonlinear partial differential equations. While scalar conservation laws, discussed in Chapter 1, exhibit complex phenomena such as shock formation and entropy solutions, systems introduce additional layers of complexity through wave interactions, characteristic families, and genuinely nonlinear behavior. This chapter develops the mathematical framework for understanding hyperbolic systems of conservation laws, with particular emphasis on the Euler equations of gas dynamics and their generalizations.

2.1 2.1 Hyperbolic Systems and the Structure of Conservation Laws

A system of conservation laws in one space dimension takes the form:

∂u/∂t + ∂f(u)/∂x = 0

where u(x,t) ∈ R^n is the vector of conserved quantities and f: R^n → R^n is the flux function. The system is hyperbolic if the Jacobian matrix A(u) = Df(u) has n real eigenvalues λ₁(u) ≤ λ₂(u) ≤ … ≤ λₙ(u) and n linearly independent eigenvectors for each u in the domain of interest.

Definition 2.1 (Hyperbolic System). The system ∂u/∂t + ∂f(u)/∂x = 0 is hyperbolic at u if the flux Jacobian A(u) = Df(u) has n real eigenvalues λ₁(u) ≤ … ≤ λₙ(u) and is diagonalizable with n linearly independent right eigenvectors r₁(u), …, rₙ(u).

The eigenvalues λᵢ(u) represent characteristic speeds—the velocities at which information propagates through the medium. The corresponding eigenvectors rᵢ(u) determine the directions in state space along which disturbances travel at these speeds.

Definition 2.2 (Genuine Nonlinearity and Linear Degeneracy). The i-th characteristic family is genuinely nonlinear if ∇λᵢ(u) · rᵢ(u) ≠ 0, and linearly degenerate if ∇λᵢ(u) · rᵢ(u) = 0 for all u.

Genuine nonlinearity leads to the formation of shock and rarefaction waves, while linear degeneracy produces contact discontinuities that preserve the characteristic speed across the jump.

2.1.1 2.1.1 The Euler Equations of Gas Dynamics

The prototypical example of a hyperbolic system is the Euler equations describing inviscid, compressible flow:

∂ρ/∂t + ∂(ρu)/∂x = 0
∂(ρu)/∂t + ∂(ρu² + p)/∂x = 0
∂E/∂t + ∂(u(E + p))/∂x = 0

Here ρ is density, u is velocity, p is pressure, and E = ρ(e + u²/2) is total energy density with e being specific internal energy. For an ideal gas, the equation of state is p = (γ-1)ρe, where γ > 1 is the adiabatic index.

The conserved variables are u = (ρ, ρu, E)ᵀ, and the flux function is:

f(u) = (ρu, ρu² + p, u(E + p))ᵀ

The flux Jacobian A(u) = Df(u) has eigenvalues λ₁ = u - c, λ₂ = u, λ₃ = u + c, where c = √(γp/ρ) is the sound speed. The first and third families (acoustic waves) are genuinely nonlinear, while the second family (contact discontinuities) is linearly degenerate.

using LinearAlgebra
using ForwardDiff
using Plots

"""
Compute primitive variables (ρ, u, p) from conservative variables (ρ, ρu, E)
for ideal gas with adiabatic index γ.
"""
function conservative_to_primitive(U, γ)
    ρ, ρu, E = U
    u = ρu / ρ
    p = (γ - 1) * (E - 0.5 * ρ * u^2)
    return [ρ, u, p]
end

"""
Compute flux vector for Euler equations.
"""
function euler_flux(U, γ)
    ρ, ρu, E = U
    u = ρu / ρ
    p = (γ - 1) * (E - 0.5 * ρ * u^2)
    
    return [ρu, ρu^2 + p, u * (E + p)]
end

"""
Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of flux Jacobian for Euler equations.
"""
function euler_eigenstructure(U, γ)
    ρ, u, p = conservative_to_primitive(U, γ)
    c = sqrt(γ * p / ρ)  # Sound speed
    H = (U[3] + p) / ρ   # Specific enthalpy
    
    # Eigenvalues (characteristic speeds)
    λ = [u - c, u, u + c]
    
    # Right eigenvectors
    R = [1.0      1.0    1.0;
         u - c    u      u + c;
         H - u*c  0.5*u^2  H + u*c]
    
    # Left eigenvectors
    L = [(0.5*(0.5*(γ-1)*u^2 + u*c))/c^2  -(0.5*(γ-1)*u + c)/c^2  0.5*(γ-1)/c^2;
         1 - 0.5*(γ-1)*u^2/c^2            (γ-1)*u/c^2             -(γ-1)/c^2;
         (0.5*(0.5*(γ-1)*u^2 - u*c))/c^2  -(0.5*(γ-1)*u - c)/c^2  0.5*(γ-1)/c^2]
    
    return λ, R, L
end

# Example: Compute eigenstructure for a specific state
γ = 1.4  # Air
U₀ = [1.0, 0.5, 2.5]  # ρ=1, ρu=0.5 (u=0.5), E=2.5
λ, R, L = euler_eigenstructure(U₀, γ)

println("Eigenvalues (characteristic speeds): ", λ)
println("Verification: L*R = I? ", norm(L*R - I) < 1e-12)

2.2 2.2 The Riemann Problem for Systems

The Riemann problem for a hyperbolic system consists of solving:

∂u/∂t + ∂f(u)/∂x = 0
u(x,0) = u_L  if x < 0
u(x,0) = u_R  if x > 0

Unlike scalar conservation laws where the solution involves a single wave, systems generate multiple waves corresponding to the different characteristic families.

Theorem 2.1 (Structure of Riemann Solutions). For a strictly hyperbolic system with n genuinely nonlinear or linearly degenerate families, the Riemann problem admits a unique entropy-satisfying solution consisting of n elementary waves separated by constant states u₀ = u_L, u₁, …, uₙ = u_R.

The elementary waves are: - Shock waves for genuinely nonlinear families when the characteristic speed decreases - Rarefaction waves for genuinely nonlinear families when the characteristic speed increases
- Contact discontinuities for linearly degenerate families

2.2.1 2.2.1 Rankine-Hugoniot Conditions for Systems

Across a shock wave traveling at speed s, the Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions are:

s(u_R - u_L) = f(u_R) - f(u_L)

For the i-th family shock connecting states u_L and u_R, there exists a parameter ε such that u_R - u_L = ε rᵢ(ū) for some intermediate state ū, and the shock speed satisfies:

s = (f(u_R) - f(u_L))/(u_R - u_L) · rᵢ(ū)

Definition 2.3 (Lax Entropy Condition for Systems). An i-th family shock satisfies the Lax entropy condition if λᵢ(u_L) > s > λᵢ(u_R) and λⱼ(u_L) ≤ s ≤ λⱼ(u_R) for j ≠ i.

2.2.2 2.2.2 Rarefaction Waves and Integral Curves

For genuinely nonlinear families, rarefaction waves are smooth solutions of the form u(x,t) = w(x/t) where w(ξ) satisfies:

(A(w) - ξI) dw/dξ = 0

This implies that w lies along an integral curve of the eigenvector field, with ξ = λᵢ(w).

Example 2.1: Riemann Problem for Euler Equations

Consider the Euler equations with γ = 1.4 and initial states: - Left: ρ_L = 1, u_L = 0, p_L = 1 - Right: ρ_R = 0.125, u_R = 0, p_R = 0.1

This is the classical Sod shock tube problem. The solution consists of a leftward rarefaction, a contact discontinuity, and a rightward shock.

using Roots

"""
Solve Riemann problem for Euler equations using exact solver.
"""
function solve_euler_riemann(ρL, uL, pL, ρR, uR, pR, γ)
    # Convert to conservative variables
    EL = pL/(γ-1) + 0.5*ρL*uL^2
    ER = pR/(γ-1) + 0.5*ρR*uR^2
    UL = [ρL, ρL*uL, EL]
    UR = [ρR, ρR*uR, ER]
    
    cL = sqrt(γ*pL/ρL)  # Left sound speed
    cR = sqrt(γ*pR/ρR)  # Right sound speed
    
    # Solve for pressure in star region using Newton iteration
    function pressure_function(p)
        # Left wave contribution
        if p > pL  # Shock
            AL = 2/((γ+1)*ρL)
            BL = (γ-1)*pL/(γ+1)
            fL = (p - pL) * sqrt(AL/(p + BL))
        else  # Rarefaction
            fL = 2*cL/(γ-1) * ((p/pL)^((γ-1)/(2*γ)) - 1)
        end
        
        # Right wave contribution  
        if p > pR  # Shock
            AR = 2/((γ+1)*ρR)
            BR = (γ-1)*pR/(γ+1)
            fR = (p - pR) * sqrt(AR/(p + BR))
        else  # Rarefaction
            fR = 2*cR/(γ-1) * ((p/pR)^((γ-1)/(2*γ)) - 1)
        end
        
        return fL + fR + (uR - uL)
    end
    
    # Initial guess for pressure
    p_guess = 0.5 * (pL + pR)
    p_star = find_zero(pressure_function, p_guess)
    
    # Compute star region velocity
    if p_star > pL
        AL = 2/((γ+1)*ρL)
        BL = (γ-1)*pL/(γ+1)
        fL = (p_star - pL) * sqrt(AL/(p_star + BL))
    else
        fL = 2*cL/(γ-1) * ((p_star/pL)^((γ-1)/(2*γ)) - 1)
    end
    
    u_star = uL + fL
    
    return p_star, u_star
end

# Solve Sod shock tube problem
p_star, u_star = solve_euler_riemann(1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 0.125, 0.0, 0.1, 1.4)
println("Star region pressure: ", p_star)
println("Star region velocity: ", u_star)

2.3 2.3 Entropy Conditions and Admissibility

The extension of entropy conditions to systems requires careful consideration of the multi-wave structure. The physical entropy for gas dynamics provides guidance, but mathematical entropy functions must be constructed for general systems.

Definition 2.4 (Entropy Pair). A pair (η, q) is an entropy pair for the system ∂u/∂t + ∂f(u)/∂x = 0 if η: R^n → R is convex and Dq = Dη · Df, where q: R^n → R is the entropy flux.

For admissible weak solutions, entropy must satisfy:

∂η(u)/∂t + ∂q(u)/∂x ≤ 0

in the sense of distributions.

2.3.1 2.3.1 Lax’s Entropy Condition

For systems, Lax’s entropy condition provides a more refined admissibility criterion than the classical Rankine-Hugoniot conditions alone.

Theorem 2.2 (Lax Entropy Condition). An i-th family shock connecting u_L to u_R is entropy-admissible if: 1. λᵢ(u_L) > σ > λᵢ(u_R) where σ is the shock speed 2. λⱼ(u_L) ≤ σ ≤ λⱼ(u_R) for all j ≠ i

This condition ensures that the shock compresses characteristics from both sides and prevents characteristic curves from crossing through the shock.

2.3.2 2.3.2 Viscous Profiles and Stability

The admissibility of shock waves can also be determined through viscous regularization. A shock is stable if it arises as the zero-viscosity limit of traveling wave solutions to the viscous system:

∂u/∂t + ∂f(u)/∂x = ε ∂²u/∂x²

Example 2.2: Viscous Profile for Euler Shocks

Consider a shock wave in the Euler equations. The viscous profile satisfies:

σ(u - u_L) + f(u) - f(u_L) = ε u'

where prime denotes d/dξ with ξ = x - σt.

using DifferentialEquations

"""
Compute viscous shock profile for Euler equations.
"""
function viscous_shock_profile(UL, UR, σ, ε, γ; ξ_span=(-10.0, 10.0))
    function profile_ode!(du, u, p, ξ)
        # Viscous profile equation: σ(u - UL) + f(u) - f(UL) = ε * du/dξ
        flux_diff = euler_flux(u, γ) - euler_flux(UL, γ)
        state_diff = σ * (u - UL)
        du .= (flux_diff + state_diff) / ε
    end
    
    # Initial condition (use tanh profile as approximation)
    u0 = 0.5 * (UL + UR)
    
    # Solve boundary value problem
    prob = ODEProblem(profile_ode!, u0, ξ_span)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), reltol=1e-8)
    
    return sol
end

# Example: Viscous profile for simple shock
UL = [1.0, 0.0, 2.5]  # Left state
UR = [0.5, 0.0, 1.8]  # Right state (compressed)
σ = -0.5  # Shock speed (leftward moving)
ε = 0.01  # Viscosity parameter

# Note: Full implementation requires sophisticated boundary value solver
println("Viscous profile computation requires specialized BVP methods")

2.4 2.4 Existence Theory and Glimm’s Scheme

The global existence of solutions to hyperbolic systems presents significant challenges compared to scalar conservation laws. The classical approach combines front-tracking methods with estimates on total variation.

2.4.1 2.4.1 Glimm’s Difference Scheme

Glimm’s scheme constructs approximate solutions by solving Riemann problems on a grid and using random sampling to handle wave interactions.

Theorem 2.3 (Glimm’s Existence Theorem). Consider a strictly hyperbolic system of conservation laws with initial data of small total variation. Then there exists a global weak solution constructed as the limit of Glimm approximations.

The scheme works as follows: 1. Discretize space-time with grid spacing Δx and time step Δt 2. At each time level, solve Riemann problems at cell interfaces 3. Use a random sequence {θₙ} ∈ [0,1] to sample the Riemann solutions 4. Prove convergence using total variation estimates

using Random

"""
Glimm's scheme for systems of conservation laws.
"""
function glimm_scheme(u0, flux_func, riemann_solver, T, Δx, Δt; 
                     random_sequence=nothing)
    N = length(u0)
    Nt = Int(ceil(T / Δt))
    
    # Initialize random sequence if not provided
    if random_sequence === nothing
        Random.seed!(42)
        random_sequence = rand(Nt)
    end
    
    u = copy(u0)
    solutions = [copy(u)]
    
    for n in 1:Nt
        u_new = similar(u)
        θ = random_sequence[n]
        
        # Solve Riemann problems at interfaces
        for i in 1:N-1
            # Solve Riemann problem between u[i] and u[i+1]
            riemann_sol = riemann_solver(u[i], u[i+1])
            
            # Sample solution at x/t = θ
            u_new[i+1] = sample_riemann_solution(riemann_sol, θ)
        end
        
        # Handle boundary conditions
        u_new[1] = u[1]  # Assumes periodic or outflow
        u_new[end] = u[end]
        
        u = u_new
        push!(solutions, copy(u))
    end
    
    return solutions
end

"""
Sample Riemann solution at ratio ξ = x/t.
This is a placeholder - actual implementation depends on system.
"""
function sample_riemann_solution(riemann_sol, ξ)
    # For illustration: return middle state for contact discontinuity
    return riemann_sol.u_middle
end

println("Glimm's scheme provides theoretical existence proof")
println("Practical implementations require system-specific Riemann solvers")

2.4.2 2.4.2 Total Variation Bounds

The key to Glimm’s existence proof is showing that approximate solutions satisfy uniform total variation bounds.

Definition 2.5 (Total Variation for Systems). The total variation of a vector function u: R → R^n is TV(u) = sup Σᵢ |u(xᵢ₊₁) - u(xᵢ)| where the supremum is over all partitions -∞ = x₀ < x₁ < … < xₙ = ∞.

For small data, wave interactions produce only second-order changes in total variation, allowing global existence.

2.5 2.5 Approximate Riemann Solvers

Exact Riemann solvers for complex systems like Euler equations are computationally expensive. Approximate solvers provide efficient alternatives while preserving essential wave structure.

2.5.1 2.5.1 Roe’s Approximate Riemann Solver

Roe’s method linearizes the flux function using a specially constructed average state.

Definition 2.6 (Roe Average). The Roe average Ā(u_L, u_R) satisfies: 1. Ā(u, u) = A(u) (consistency) 2. Ā(u_L, u_R)(u_R - u_L) = f(u_R) - f(u_L) (conservation) 3. Ā(u_L, u_R) has real eigenvalues and complete eigenvector set

For the Euler equations, the Roe average uses density-weighted arithmetic means:

ρ̄ = √(ρ_L ρ_R)
ū = (√ρ_L u_L + √ρ_R u_R)/(√ρ_L + √ρ_R)
H̄ = (√ρ_L H_L + √ρ_R H_R)/(√ρ_L + √ρ_R)
"""
Roe approximate Riemann solver for Euler equations.
"""
function roe_solver(UL, UR, γ)
    # Convert to primitive variables
    ρL, uL, pL = conservative_to_primitive(UL, γ)
    ρR, uR, pR = conservative_to_primitive(UR, γ)
    
    # Compute specific enthalpies
    HL = (UL[3] + pL) / ρL
    HR = (UR[3] + pR) / ρR
    
    # Roe averages
    sqrt_ρL = sqrt(ρL)
    sqrt_ρR = sqrt(ρR)
    sqrt_ρ_sum = sqrt_ρL + sqrt_ρR
    
    ρ_roe = sqrt_ρL * sqrt_ρR
    u_roe = (sqrt_ρL * uL + sqrt_ρR * uR) / sqrt_ρ_sum
    H_roe = (sqrt_ρL * HL + sqrt_ρR * HR) / sqrt_ρ_sum
    
    # Roe sound speed
    c_roe = sqrt((γ - 1) * (H_roe - 0.5 * u_roe^2))
    
    # Roe eigenvalues
    λ_roe = [u_roe - c_roe, u_roe, u_roe + c_roe]
    
    # Wave strengths (coefficients of eigenvector decomposition)
    Δρ = ρR - ρL
    Δu = uR - uL
    Δp = pR - pL
    
    # Compute wave strengths
    α2 = Δρ - Δp / c_roe^2
    α1 = 0.5 * (Δp / ρ_roe / c_roe^2 - Δu) / c_roe
    α3 = 0.5 * (Δp / ρ_roe / c_roe^2 + Δu) / c_roe
    
    α = [α1, α2, α3]
    
    # Right eigenvectors at Roe state
    R_roe = [1.0         1.0    1.0;
             u_roe - c_roe   u_roe  u_roe + c_roe;
             H_roe - u_roe*c_roe  0.5*u_roe^2  H_roe + u_roe*c_roe]
    
    return λ_roe, α, R_roe
end

# Example: Apply Roe solver to Sod problem
UL_sod = [1.0, 0.0, 2.5]
UR_sod = [0.125, 0.0, 0.225]
λ, α, R = roe_solver(UL_sod, UR_sod, 1.4)

println("Roe eigenvalues: ", λ)
println("Wave strengths: ", α)

2.5.2 2.5.2 HLL and HLLC Solvers

The HLL (Harten, Lax, van Leer) solver uses only the fastest left-going and right-going wave speeds.

Definition 2.7 (HLL Flux). The HLL flux is:

f_HLL = (S_R f(u_L) - S_L f(u_R) + S_L S_R (u_R - u_L))/(S_R - S_L)

where S_L ≤ 0 ≤ S_R are estimates of the fastest wave speeds.

The HLLC solver adds resolution of the contact discontinuity for gas dynamics.

"""
HLL approximate Riemann solver.
"""
function hll_solver(UL, UR, γ)
    # Primitive variables
    ρL, uL, pL = conservative_to_primitive(UL, γ)
    ρR, uR, pR = conservative_to_primitive(UR, γ)
    
    cL = sqrt(γ * pL / ρL)
    cR = sqrt(γ * pR / ρR)
    
    # Wave speed estimates (simple approach)
    SL = min(uL - cL, uR - cR)
    SR = max(uL + cL, uR + cR)
    
    # HLL flux
    fL = euler_flux(UL, γ)
    fR = euler_flux(UR, γ)
    
    if SL >= 0
        return fL
    elseif SR <= 0
        return fR
    else
        f_hll = (SR * fL - SL * fR + SL * SR * (UR - UL)) / (SR - SL)
        return f_hll
    end
end

"""
HLLC solver with contact wave resolution.
"""
function hllc_solver(UL, UR, γ)
    # Get HLL wave speeds
    ρL, uL, pL = conservative_to_primitive(UL, γ)
    ρR, uR, pR = conservative_to_primitive(UR, γ)
    
    cL = sqrt(γ * pL / ρL)
    cR = sqrt(γ * pR / ρR)
    
    SL = min(uL - cL, uR - cR)
    SR = max(uL + cL, uR + cR)
    
    # Contact speed
    SM = (pR - pL + ρL*uL*(SL - uL) - ρR*uR*(SR - uR)) / (ρL*(SL - uL) - ρR*(SR - uR))
    
    # Intermediate states
    function compute_star_state(U, S, SM, γ)
        ρ, u, p = conservative_to_primitive(U, γ)
        ρ_star = ρ * (S - u) / (S - SM)
        E_star = ρ_star * (U[3]/ρ + (SM - u)*(SM + p/(ρ*(S - u))))
        return [ρ_star, ρ_star * SM, E_star]
    end
    
    fL = euler_flux(UL, γ)
    fR = euler_flux(UR, γ)
    
    if SL >= 0
        return fL
    elseif SM >= 0
        UL_star = compute_star_state(UL, SL, SM, γ)
        return fL + SL * (UL_star - UL)
    elseif SR >= 0
        UR_star = compute_star_state(UR, SR, SM, γ)
        return fR + SR * (UR_star - UR)
    else
        return fR
    end
end

# Compare flux computations
f_exact = euler_flux(0.5 * (UL_sod + UR_sod), 1.4)  # Approximate
f_hll = hll_solver(UL_sod, UR_sod, 1.4)
f_hllc = hllc_solver(UL_sod, UR_sod, 1.4)

println("Flux comparison (mass component):")
println("HLL:  ", f_hll[1])
println("HLLC: ", f_hllc[1])

2.6 2.6 Applications to Physical Systems

2.6.1 2.6.1 Shallow Water Equations

The shallow water equations model flow in rivers, coastal regions, and atmospheric dynamics:

∂h/∂t + ∂(hu)/∂x = 0
∂(hu)/∂t + ∂(hu² + ½gh²)/∂x = 0

Here h is water depth, u is velocity, and g is gravitational acceleration. The system has eigenvalues λ₁ = u - √(gh), λ₂ = u + √(gh), both genuinely nonlinear.

2.6.2 2.6.2 Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD)

The ideal MHD equations describe plasma dynamics:

∂ρ/∂t + ∂(ρu)/∂x = 0
∂(ρu)/∂t + ∂(ρu² + p + B²/2)/∂x = 0
∂B/∂t + ∂(uB)/∂x = 0
∂E/∂t + ∂(u(E + p + B²/2))/∂x = 0

The magnetic field B introduces additional wave families: fast and slow magnetosonic waves, Alfvén waves, and entropy waves.

"""
Shallow water system eigenstructure.
"""
function shallow_water_eigenstructure(h, u, g)
    c = sqrt(g * h)  # Shallow water wave speed
    
    # Conservative variables: U = [h, hu]
    # Eigenvalues
    λ = [u - c, u + c]
    
    # Right eigenvectors
    R = [1.0    1.0;
         u - c  u + c]
    
    # Left eigenvectors
    L = [0.5*(u + c)/c  -0.5/c;
         0.5*(-u + c)/c  0.5/c]
    
    return λ, R, L
end

# Example: Dam break problem for shallow water
h_left, u_left = 2.0, 0.0    # Deep water at rest
h_right, u_right = 1.0, 0.0  # Shallow water at rest
g = 9.81

λ_sw, R_sw, L_sw = shallow_water_eigenstructure(0.5*(h_left + h_right), 
                                               0.5*(u_left + u_right), g)
println("Shallow water eigenvalues: ", λ_sw)

2.7 2.7 Numerical Methods for Systems

2.7.1 2.7.1 Finite Volume Methods

Finite volume schemes for systems follow the same structure as scalar methods but require matrix flux computations:

u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - (Δt/Δx)(F_{i+1/2}^n - F_{i-1/2}^n)

where F_{i+1/2}^n is the numerical flux

3 Chapter 3: Reaction-Diffusion Systems

3.1 3.1 Introduction to Reaction-Diffusion Systems

Reaction-diffusion systems model the evolution of chemical species, biological populations, or other quantities that undergo local reactions and spatial diffusion. The general form of a reaction-diffusion system for a vector of concentrations u = (u₁, u₂, …, uₙ) is:

∂u/∂t = D∇²u + f(u)

where D is a matrix of diffusion coefficients and f(u) represents the reaction kinetics. These systems exhibit remarkably rich dynamics: traveling waves, stationary patterns, spiral waves, and chaotic spatiotemporal behavior.

The mathematical theory of reaction-diffusion systems draws upon techniques from dynamical systems, functional analysis, and asymptotic methods. Unlike linear diffusion equations studied in PDE I, the nonlinearity in f(u) can lead to phenomena impossible in linear systems, including the spontaneous generation of spatial structure through Turing instability.

3.2 3.2 The Fisher-KPP Equation

3.2.1 3.2.1 Derivation and Properties

The Fisher-Kolmogorov-Petrovskii-Piskunov (Fisher-KPP) equation is the canonical example of a scalar reaction-diffusion equation:

∂u/∂t = D∂²u/∂x² + ru(1 - u/K)

where D > 0 is the diffusion coefficient, r > 0 is the intrinsic growth rate, and K > 0 is the carrying capacity. Rescaling time by r and space by √(D/r), we obtain the normalized form:

∂u/∂t = ∂²u/∂x² + u(1 - u)

Definition 3.1 (Fisher-KPP Equation). The Fisher-KPP equation is the nonlinear reaction-diffusion equation ∂u/∂t = ∂²u/∂x² + u(1 - u), where u(x,t) represents population density or gene frequency.

The reaction term f(u) = u(1 - u) has equilibria at u = 0 (unstable) and u = 1 (stable). The Fisher-KPP equation models the spread of an advantageous gene or the expansion of a biological population into empty territory.

3.2.2 3.2.2 Traveling Wave Solutions

We seek traveling wave solutions of the form u(x,t) = U(ξ) where ξ = x - ct for some wave speed c > 0. Substituting into the Fisher-KPP equation yields the ordinary differential equation:

-cU’ = U’’ + U(1 - U)

with boundary conditions U(-∞) = 1 and U(∞) = 0.

Theorem 3.1 (Fisher-KPP Traveling Waves). The Fisher-KPP equation admits traveling wave solutions u(x,t) = U(x - ct) connecting the states u = 1 and u = 0 if and only if c ≥ c* = 2, where c* is the minimum wave speed.

Proof. The traveling wave ODE can be written as a system:

U' = V
V' = -cV - U(1 - U)

At the equilibrium (0,0), linearization gives the characteristic equation λ² + cλ + 1 = 0 with roots λ = (-c ± √(c² - 4))/2.

For c > 2, both roots are real and negative, allowing a heteroclinic connection from (1,0) to (0,0). For c < 2, the roots are complex, and no monotonic traveling wave exists.

The critical case c = 2 corresponds to the minimum wave speed. For c = c*, we have λ = -1 (double root), and the traveling wave profile satisfies:

U(ξ) = (1 + ξ/2)²e^(-ξ/2) for ξ > 0 □

3.2.3 3.2.3 Asymptotic Analysis of Wave Fronts

The traveling wave profile for c > c* can be analyzed asymptotically. As ξ → ∞, U(ξ) ~ Ae^(λ₁ξ) where λ₁ = (-c + √(c² - 4))/2 < 0. As ξ → -∞, the solution approaches U = 1 exponentially.

Example 3.1 (Numerical Solution of Fisher-KPP Waves). We solve the Fisher-KPP equation numerically and compare traveling wave solutions:

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Fisher-KPP equation
function fisher_kpp!(du, u, p, t)
    D, r = p
    n = length(u)
    dx = 20.0 / (n - 1)  # Domain [-10, 10]
    
    # Interior points
    for i in 2:n-1
        du[i] = D * (u[i+1] - 2*u[i] + u[i-1]) / dx^2 + r * u[i] * (1 - u[i])
    end
    
    # Boundary conditions (Neumann)
    du[1] = du[2]
    du[n] = du[n-1]
end

# Initial condition: step function
n = 200
x = range(-10, 10, length=n)
u0 = [xi < 0 ? 1.0 : 0.0 for xi in x]

# Parameters: D = 1, r = 1 (normalized)
p = [1.0, 1.0]
tspan = (0.0, 10.0)

# Solve
prob = ODEProblem(fisher_kpp!, u0, tspan, p)
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())

# Plot solution at different times
plot_times = [0, 2, 5, 10]
p1 = plot(xlabel="x", ylabel="u", title="Fisher-KPP Wave Propagation")
for t in plot_times
    u_t = sol(t)
    plot!(p1, x, u_t, label="t=$t", lw=2)
end

# Theoretical minimum wave speed
c_min = 2 * sqrt(1.0 * 1.0)  # c* = 2√(Dr)
println("Minimum wave speed: c* = $c_min")

3.3 3.3 Bistable Reaction-Diffusion Equations

3.3.1 3.3.1 The Bistable Nonlinearity

Consider the reaction-diffusion equation with bistable kinetics:

∂u/∂t = ∂²u/∂x² + f(u)

where f(u) = u(1-u)(u-a) with 0 < a < 1. This cubic nonlinearity has three equilibria: u = 0 and u = 1 (stable) and u = a (unstable).

Definition 3.2 (Bistable Equation). A reaction-diffusion equation ∂u/∂t = ∂²u/∂x² + f(u) is bistable if f has exactly two stable equilibria separated by an unstable equilibrium.

The bistable equation models phase transitions, where u represents the local phase (e.g., liquid vs. solid, or different biological phenotypes).

3.3.2 3.3.2 Traveling Front Solutions

Unlike the Fisher-KPP case, bistable equations have a unique wave speed for traveling fronts connecting the stable states.

Theorem 3.2 (Bistable Wave Speed). For the bistable equation ∂u/∂t = ∂²u/∂x² + u(1-u)(u-a), there exists a unique wave speed c(a) such that traveling wave solutions u(x,t) = U(x-ct) exist connecting u = 0 to u = 1.

The wave speed can be computed as:

c(a) = -∫₀¹ f(u) du / ∫₀¹ U’(ξ)² dξ

For a = 1/2, we have c = 0 (stationary front). For a ≠ 1/2, the front propagates toward the less stable state.

Example 3.2 (Bistable Front Dynamics). We simulate bistable fronts with different values of a:

# Bistable reaction term
function bistable_reaction(u, a)
    return u * (1 - u) * (u - a)
end

# Bistable equation solver
function bistable_pde!(du, u, p, t)
    a = p[1]
    n = length(u)
    dx = 40.0 / (n - 1)  # Domain [-20, 20]
    
    for i in 2:n-1
        du[i] = (u[i+1] - 2*u[i] + u[i-1]) / dx^2 + bistable_reaction(u[i], a)
    end
    
    # Fixed boundary conditions
    du[1] = 0
    du[n] = 0
end

# Compare different bistability parameters
x = range(-20, 20, length=400)
u0 = [tanh((xi + 5) / 2) / 2 + 0.5 for xi in x]  # Initial front

fig = plot(layout=(2, 2), size=(800, 600))

for (i, a) in enumerate([0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7])
    prob = ODEProblem(bistable_pde!, u0, (0.0, 20.0), [a])
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())
    
    plot!(fig[i], x, sol(0), label="t=0", alpha=0.7)
    plot!(fig[i], x, sol(10), label="t=10", alpha=0.7)
    plot!(fig[i], x, sol(20), label="t=20", lw=2)
    title!(fig[i], "a = $a")
    xlabel!(fig[i], "x")
    ylabel!(fig[i], "u")
end

display(fig)

3.4 3.4 Turing Instability and Pattern Formation

3.4.1 3.4.1 Linear Stability Analysis

Consider a two-component reaction-diffusion system:

∂u/∂t = D₁∇²u + f(u,v) ∂v/∂t = D₂∇²v + g(u,v)

Let (u₀, v₀) be a spatially homogeneous equilibrium. To analyze stability, we consider small perturbations of the form:

(u,v) = (u₀,v₀) + ε(û,v̂)e^(σt + ik·x)

Substituting into the linearized system yields the dispersion relation:

det(J - D k² I - σI) = 0

where J is the Jacobian matrix of (f,g) at (u₀,v₀) and D = diag(D₁,D₂).

Definition 3.3 (Turing Instability). A spatially homogeneous equilibrium is Turing unstable if it is stable to spatially uniform perturbations but unstable to spatially periodic perturbations of some wavelength.

Theorem 3.3 (Turing Instability Conditions). A two-component system exhibits Turing instability if: 1. The equilibrium is stable without diffusion: tr(J) < 0, det(J) > 0 2. One diffusion coefficient is much larger than the other: D₂ >> D₁ 3. The cross-reaction terms satisfy: f_v < 0, g_u > 0 (activator-inhibitor structure)

Proof. The characteristic polynomial is σ² - σ tr(J - Dk²) + det(J - Dk²) = 0. For instability, we need Re(σ) > 0 for some k. The critical wave number k_c minimizes the discriminant, and instability occurs when det(J - Dk_c²) < 0. This requires the activator-inhibitor structure and differential diffusion. □

3.4.2 3.4.2 The Gierer-Meinhardt Model

A classical activator-inhibitor system is the Gierer-Meinhardt model:

∂A/∂t = D_A ∇²A + μA²/H - νA + σ ∂H/∂t = D_H ∇²H + ρA² - δH

where A is the activator, H is the inhibitor, D_A << D_H, and all parameters are positive.

Example 3.3 (Turing Patterns in the Gierer-Meinhardt Model). We simulate pattern formation from random initial conditions:

using FFTW

# Gierer-Meinhardt parameters
μ, ν, σ = 0.02, 0.2, 0.001
ρ, δ = 0.02, 0.1
D_A, D_H = 1.0, 20.0

# Domain and discretization
Lx, Ly = 50.0, 50.0
nx, ny = 128, 128
dx, dy = Lx/(nx-1), Ly/(ny-1)

# Wavenumber grids for spectral differentiation
kx = fftfreq(nx, nx/Lx) * 2π
ky = fftfreq(ny, ny/Ly) * 2π
Kx, Ky = meshgrid(kx, ky)
K2 = Kx.^2 + Ky.^2

function gierer_meinhardt!(du, u, p, t)
    A = u[:, :, 1]
    H = u[:, :, 2]
    
    # Reaction terms
    R_A = μ * A.^2 ./ H - ν * A .+ σ
    R_H = ρ * A.^2 - δ * H
    
    # Spectral diffusion
    A_hat = fft(A)
    H_hat = fft(H)
    
    du[:, :, 1] = real(ifft(-D_A * K2 .* A_hat)) + R_A
    du[:, :, 2] = real(ifft(-D_H * K2 .* H_hat)) + R_H
end

# Initial conditions: small random perturbations around equilibrium
A_eq = sqrt(δ*σ/(μ-ν*δ/ρ))  # Equilibrium values
H_eq = ρ*A_eq^2/δ

A0 = A_eq * (1 .+ 0.1*randn(nx, ny))
H0 = H_eq * (1 .+ 0.1*randn(nx, ny))
u0 = cat(A0, H0, dims=3)

# Solve
prob = ODEProblem(gierer_meinhardt!, u0, (0.0, 500.0), [])
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), reltol=1e-6)

# Plot final pattern
x = range(0, Lx, length=nx)
y = range(0, Ly, length=ny)
A_final = sol.u[end][:, :, 1]

heatmap(x, y, A_final', 
        title="Turing Pattern (Activator)", 
        xlabel="x", ylabel="y", 
        color=:viridis, aspect_ratio=1)

3.5 3.5 Spiral Waves and Excitable Media

3.5.1 3.5.1 Excitable Systems

An excitable system has three characteristic states: 1. Rest state: Stable equilibrium 2. Excited state: Large-amplitude excursion from rest 3. Refractory state: Period of reduced excitability

The FitzHugh-Nagumo model is a paradigmatic example:

∂u/∂t = D∇²u + u - u³/3 - v + I ∂v/∂t = ε(u + a - bv)

where u is the fast activator (membrane potential), v is the slow inhibitor (recovery variable), and ε << 1.

Definition 3.4 (Excitable Medium). A reaction-diffusion system is excitable if: (1) it has a stable rest state, (2) sufficiently large perturbations trigger a large-amplitude excursion before returning to rest, and (3) there exists a refractory period during which the system cannot be excited.

3.5.2 3.5.2 Spiral Wave Dynamics

In two spatial dimensions, excitable media can support rotating spiral waves. These are solutions of the form u(x,y,t) = U(r,θ - ωt) in polar coordinates, where ω is the angular frequency.

The spiral tip traces a circular or meandering trajectory, and the wavelength λ and frequency ω satisfy the dispersion relation of the medium. Spiral waves are observed in chemical reactions (Belousov-Zhabotinsky), cardiac tissue, and neural networks.

Example 3.4 (FitzHugh-Nagumo Spiral Waves). We simulate spiral wave formation and dynamics:

# FitzHugh-Nagumo parameters
a, b, ε = 0.7, 0.8, 0.08
I = 0.0  # No external current
D = 1.0

function fitzhugh_nagumo!(du, u, p, t)
    nx, ny = size(u)[1:2]
    dx, dy = 1.0, 1.0  # Grid spacing
    
    u_var = u[:, :, 1]  # Fast variable
    v_var = u[:, :, 2]  # Slow variable
    
    # Laplacian with periodic boundary conditions
    laplacian_u = zeros(nx, ny)
    for i in 1:nx
        for j in 1:ny
            im1 = i == 1 ? nx : i-1
            ip1 = i == nx ? 1 : i+1
            jm1 = j == 1 ? ny : j-1
            jp1 = j == ny ? 1 : j+1
            
            laplacian_u[i,j] = (u_var[ip1,j] + u_var[im1,j] - 2*u_var[i,j])/dx^2 + 
                               (u_var[i,jp1] + u_var[i,jm1] - 2*u_var[i,j])/dy^2
        end
    end
    
    # Reaction terms
    du[:, :, 1] = D * laplacian_u + u_var - u_var.^3/3 - v_var .+ I
    du[:, :, 2] = ε * (u_var .+ a - b * v_var)
end

# Initialize with broken wave front to generate spiral
nx, ny = 100, 100
u0 = zeros(nx, ny, 2)

# Create initial excitation pattern
for i in 1:nx, j in 1:ny
    if i < nx/2 && j < ny/2
        u0[i, j, 1] = 1.0  # Excited region
    end
end

# Add break in the wave front
u0[45:50, :, 1] .= -1.0

prob = ODEProblem(fitzhugh_nagumo!, u0, (0.0, 100.0), [])
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=5.0)

# Create animation of spiral wave
anim = @animate for i in 1:length(sol.t)
    heatmap(sol.u[i][:, :, 1], 
           title="FitzHugh-Nagumo t=$(sol.t[i])", 
           clim=(-1.5, 1.5), 
           color=:RdBu, aspect_ratio=1)
end

gif(anim, "spiral_wave.gif", fps=5)

3.6 3.6 Applications to Morphogenesis and Ecology

3.6.1 3.6.1 Morphogen Gradients and Development

Reaction-diffusion systems provide mechanisms for biological pattern formation during development. The French Flag model proposes that cells differentiate according to local morphogen concentration:

∂m/∂t = D_m ∇²m + f(m) - γm

where m(x,t) is morphogen concentration, f(m) represents autocatalytic production, and γ is the decay rate.

Morphogen gradients can be established by localized production and uniform decay, creating spatial information for cellular differentiation. The length scale of the gradient is determined by √(D_m/γ).

3.6.2 3.6.2 Ecological Spatial Dynamics

In ecology, reaction-diffusion models describe population dynamics with migration. The classical predator-prey system with diffusion is:

∂N/∂t = D_N ∇²N + rN(1 - N/K) - aNP/(1 + hN) ∂P/∂t = D_P ∇²P + eaNP/(1 + hN) - dP

where N is prey density, P is predator density, and the parameters represent growth rates, predation efficiency, and handling time.

Spatial structure can stabilize predator-prey oscillations, create traveling waves of invasion, or generate patchy distributions through pattern formation.

Example 3.5 (Spatial Predator-Prey Dynamics). We model the spatial Rosenzweig-MacArthur system:

# Rosenzweig-MacArthur parameters
r, K = 1.0, 1.0      # Prey growth rate and carrying capacity
a, h = 2.0, 1.0      # Attack rate and handling time
e, d = 0.5, 0.1      # Conversion efficiency and predator death
D_N, D_P = 0.01, 0.005  # Diffusion coefficients

function rosenzweig_macarthur_spatial!(du, u, p, t)
    N = u[:, :, 1]  # Prey
    P = u[:, :, 2]  # Predator
    
    nx, ny = size(N)
    dx, dy = 0.5, 0.5
    
    # Compute Laplacians
    ∇²N = zeros(nx, ny)
    ∇²P = zeros(nx, ny)
    
    for i in 2:nx-1, j in 2:ny-1
        ∇²N[i,j] = (N[i+1,j] + N[i-1,j] + N[i,j+1] + N[i,j-1] - 4*N[i,j])/(dx^2)
        ∇²P[i,j] = (P[i+1,j] + P[i-1,j] + P[i,j+1] + P[i,j-1] - 4*P[i,j])/(dx^2)
    end
    
    # Predation term
    predation = a * N .* P ./ (1 .+ h * N)
    
    # Population dynamics
    du[:, :, 1] = D_N * ∇²N + r * N .* (1 .- N/K) - predation
    du[:, :, 2] = D_P * ∇²P + e * predation - d * P
end

# Initial conditions: random spatial distribution
nx, ny = 50, 50
N0 = 0.5 * (1 .+ 0.2 * randn(nx, ny))
P0 = 0.3 * (1 .+ 0.2 * randn(nx, ny))
u0 = cat(max.(N0, 0), max.(P0, 0), dims=3)

prob = ODEProblem(rosenzweig_macarthur_spatial!, u0, (0.0, 200.0), [])
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=10.0)

# Plot final distribution
N_final = sol.u[end][:, :, 1]
P_final = sol.u[end][:, :, 2]

p1 = heatmap(N_final, title="Prey Density", color=:viridis)
p2 = heatmap(P_final, title="Predator Density", color=:plasma)
plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(800, 300))

3.7 3.7 Advanced Topics: Neural Field Equations

Neural field equations model the coarse-grained dynamics of neural tissue through integro-differential equations:

∂u/∂t = -u + ∫ w(x-y) f(u(y,t)) dy + h(x,t)

where u(x,t) represents neural activity, w(x-y) is the spatial coupling kernel, f is the activation function, and h(x,t) is external input.

When the coupling kernel is sufficiently localized, the integral can be approximated by a reaction-diffusion equation:

∂u/∂t = D∇²u - u + f(u) + h(x,t)

Neural field equations exhibit traveling waves (propagating activity), stationary patterns (hallucination patterns), and spiral waves (cortical spreading depression).

3.8 Exercises

Exercise 3.1 (Computational). Implement the Fisher-KPP equation with different initial conditions and verify numerically that the minimum wave speed is c* = 2. Plot the wave profiles for c = 2.1, 2.5, and 3.0.

Exercise 3.2 (Theoretical). Prove that the Fisher-KPP equation u_t = u_xx + u(1-u) has a comparison principle: if u₁(x,0) ≤ u₂(x,0) for all x and u₁, u₂ satisfy appropriate boundary conditions, then u₁(x,t) ≤ u₂(x,t) for all x,t > 0.

Exercise 3.3 (Computational). Simulate the bistable equation with a = 0.3 and compute the wave speed numerically. Compare with the theoretical formula.

Exercise 3.4 (Theoretical). For the Gierer-Meinhardt system, derive the conditions for Turing instability by analyzing the linearized system around the homogeneous steady state.

Exercise 3.5 (Computational). Modify the FitzHugh-Nagumo simulation to study spiral wave breakup by increasing the parameter ε. At what value does the single spiral become unstable?

Exercise 3.6 (Theoretical). Consider the reaction-diffusion system u_t = u_xx + u - u³, v_t = D v_xx + u - v. Show that if D is large enough, the system exhibits Turing instability.

Exercise 3.7 (Computational). Implement a three-species competition model with diffusion and explore the formation of spatial domains. Use the Lotka-Volterra competition dynamics with appropriate parameter values.

Exercise 3.8 (Theoretical). Prove that traveling wave solutions of the bistable equation are unique up to translation when they exist.

Exercise 3.9 (Computational). Simulate pattern formation in the Gray-Scott model: u_t = D_u ∇²u - uv² + F(1-u), v_t = D_v ∇²v + uv² - (F+k)v. Explore different parameter regimes (F,k) to find spots, stripes, and chaotic patterns.

Exercise 3.10 (Advanced Theoretical). Consider the Allen-Cahn equation u_t = ε²∇²u + u - u³. Using matched asymptotic expansions, derive the motion law for interfaces in the limit ε → 0. Show that interfaces move with normal velocity proportional to their mean curvature.

3.9 Connections

Reaction-diffusion systems bridge multiple areas of the PDE curriculum. The traveling wave analysis connects to dynamical systems theory and phase plane methods. The pattern formation mechanisms link to bifurcation theory and weakly nonlinear analysis. Turing instability analysis employs techniques from linear algebra and spectral theory that appear throughout the study of PDEs.

The mathematical tools developed here—comparison principles, energy methods, and asymptotic analysis—are fundamental for studying other nonlinear PDEs including Hamilton-Jacobi equations (Chapter 4) and the Navier-Stokes equations (Chapter 5). The spatial pattern formation mechanisms are also relevant for understanding free boundary problems (Chapter 6) where interfaces separate different phases.

From an applications perspective, reaction-diffusion systems provide mathematical models for phenomena across biology, chemistry, ecology, and neuroscience. The interplay between local nonlinear dynamics and spatial coupling generates emergent collective behavior that is impossible in purely temporal or purely spatial systems.

3.10 References

Fisher, R.A. (1937). The wave of advance of advantageous genes. Annals of Eugenics, 7(4), 355-369.

Kolmogorov, A., Petrovskii, I., & Piskunov, N. (1937). A study of the diffusion equation with increase in the amount of substance, and its application to a biological problem. Moscow University Mathematics Bulletin, 1(6), 1-26.

Turing, A.M. (1952). The chemical basis of morphogenesis. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London B, 237(641), 37-72.

Murray, J.D. (2002). Mathematical Biology I: An Introduction, 3rd edition. Springer-Verlag.

FitzHugh, R. (1961). Impulses and physiological states in theoretical models of nerve membrane. Biophysical Journal, 1(6), 445-466.

Nagumo, J., Arimoto, S., & Yoshizawa, S. (1962). An active pulse transmission line simulating nerve axon. Proceedings of the IRE, 50(10), 2061-2070.

Gierer, A., & Meinhardt, H. (1972). A theory of biological pattern formation. Kybernetik, 12(1), 30-39.

Keener, J., & Sneyd, J. (2009). Mathematical Physiology I: Cellular Physiology, 2nd edition. Springer-Verlag.

Cross, M., & Hohenberg, P. (1993). Pattern formation outside of equilibrium. Reviews of Modern Physics, 65(3), 851-1112.

4 Chapter 4: Hamilton-Jacobi Equations and Viscosity Solutions

Hamilton-Jacobi equations represent a fundamental class of first-order nonlinear partial differential equations that arise naturally in optimal control theory, classical mechanics, differential games, and front propagation problems. Unlike the hyperbolic conservation laws studied in previous chapters, Hamilton-Jacobi equations exhibit unique mathematical challenges: classical solutions typically cease to exist in finite time due to the formation of singularities in the gradient, yet the underlying physical or control-theoretic problems remain well-posed. The theory of viscosity solutions, developed by Crandall and Lions in the 1980s, provides the appropriate mathematical framework for understanding these equations in their full generality.

4.1 4.1 The Hamilton-Jacobi Equation and Classical Solutions

The general form of a Hamilton-Jacobi equation is:

\[u_t + H(x, t, u, Du) = 0\]

where \(u: \mathbb{R}^n \times [0,T) \to \mathbb{R}\) is the unknown function, \(Du = \nabla_x u\) denotes the spatial gradient, and \(H: \mathbb{R}^n \times [0,T) \times \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}\) is the Hamiltonian function. We typically prescribe initial data \(u(x,0) = u_0(x)\).

4.1.1 4.1.1 The Eikonal Equation

A prototypical example is the eikonal equation:

\[|Du| = n(x)\]

where \(n(x) > 0\) represents the refractive index of the medium. This equation arises in geometric optics as a high-frequency limit of the wave equation, describing the propagation of wave fronts. The level sets \(\{x : u(x) = c\}\) represent wave fronts at different times.

Example 4.1 (Isotropic Eikonal). Consider the homogeneous eikonal equation \(|Du| = 1\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) with initial condition \(u(x,0) = |x|\). The classical solution is:

\[u(x,t) = |x| - t \quad \text{for } t < |x|\]

This represents wave fronts expanding inward toward the origin. The solution becomes undefined when \(t = |x|\), corresponding to the focusing of characteristics at the origin.

4.1.2 4.1.2 Connection to Optimal Control

Hamilton-Jacobi equations emerge naturally from dynamic programming principles in optimal control. Consider the control problem:

\[\text{minimize } \int_0^T L(x(s), a(s)) \, ds + g(x(T))\]

subject to \(\dot{x}(s) = f(x(s), a(s))\) with \(x(0) = y\). The value function \(u(y,t) = \inf_{a(\cdot)} J[y,t;a(\cdot)]\) satisfies the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation:

\[u_t + \inf_{a \in A} \{L(x,a) + Du \cdot f(x,a)\} = 0\]

with terminal condition \(u(x,T) = g(x)\).

Theorem 4.1 (Dynamic Programming Principle). If the value function \(u\) is continuously differentiable, then it satisfies the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation above.

Proof. The proof follows from the optimality principle: for any \(h > 0\) small,

\[u(y,t) = \inf_{a(\cdot)} \left\{ \int_t^{t+h} L(x(s),a(s)) \, ds + u(x(t+h), t+h) \right\}\]

Expanding \(u(x(t+h), t+h)\) to first order and taking the limit as \(h \to 0\) yields the desired equation. □

4.1.3 4.1.3 Method of Characteristics

For classical solutions, we can solve Hamilton-Jacobi equations using the method of characteristics. The characteristic equations are:

\[\begin{align} \dot{x} &= H_p(x,t,u,p) \\ \dot{p} &= -H_x(x,t,u,p) - H_u(x,t,u,p) p \\ \dot{u} &= p \cdot H_p(x,t,u,p) - H(x,t,u,p) \end{align}\]

with initial conditions \(x(0) = y\), \(p(0) = Du_0(y)\), \(u(0) = u_0(y)\).

Example 4.2 (Burgers-Type Hamilton-Jacobi). Consider \(u_t + \frac{1}{2}|Du|^2 = 0\) with \(u(x,0) = x^2/2\). The characteristic equations become:

\[\dot{x} = p, \quad \dot{p} = 0, \quad \dot{u} = p^2/2\]

with initial conditions \(p(0) = x_0\), \(u(0) = x_0^2/2\). The solution along characteristics is:

\[x(t) = x_0 + x_0 t = x_0(1+t), \quad u(t) = \frac{x_0^2}{2}(1+t)\]

Inverting to get \(x_0 = x/(1+t)\), we obtain the classical solution:

\[u(x,t) = \frac{x^2}{2(1+t)} \quad \text{for } t > -1\]

The solution develops a gradient blow-up as \(t \to -1^+\).

4.2 4.2 Breakdown of Classical Solutions

A fundamental difficulty with Hamilton-Jacobi equations is that classical solutions typically exist only for short times, even with smooth initial data. This breakdown occurs when characteristics intersect, creating multi-valued solutions.

Theorem 4.2 (Finite-Time Breakdown). Consider the Hamilton-Jacobi equation \(u_t + H(Du) = 0\) where \(H\) is convex and coercive. If the initial data \(u_0\) is not convex, then classical solutions generally cease to exist in finite time.

The breakdown mechanism is fundamentally different from the shock formation in conservation laws studied in Chapter 1. In Hamilton-Jacobi equations, it is the gradient \(Du\) that becomes discontinuous, while the solution \(u\) itself may remain continuous.

using LinearAlgebra, Plots

function characteristic_solution_burgers_hj(x0_vals, t_vals)
    """
    Compute characteristic solutions for u_t + (1/2)|∇u|^2 = 0
    with initial condition u(x,0) = x^2/2
    """
    solutions = []
    
    for t in t_vals
        if t ≥ -1
            x_vals = x0_vals .* (1 + t)
            u_vals = (x0_vals.^2 / 2) .* (1 + t)
            push!(solutions, (x_vals, u_vals, t))
        end
    end
    
    return solutions
end

# Visualize characteristic breakdown
x0_range = -2:0.1:2
t_values = [-0.8, -0.5, -0.2, 0.0, 0.3]

plots = []
for (i, t) in enumerate(t_values)
    if t > -1
        x_vals = x0_range .* (1 + t)
        u_vals = (x0_range.^2 / 2) .* (1 + t)
        
        p = plot(x_vals, u_vals, linewidth=2, 
                title="t = $t", xlabel="x", ylabel="u",
                legend=false, xlim=(-3, 3), ylim=(0, 2))
        push!(plots, p)
    end
end

# [FIGURE: Evolution of characteristic solutions showing gradient blow-up as t approaches -1]
plot(plots..., layout=(1, length(plots)), size=(1200, 300))

4.3 4.3 Viscosity Solutions: Definition and Basic Properties

The theory of viscosity solutions provides a weak solution concept that captures the correct physical behavior of Hamilton-Jacobi equations even after classical solutions break down.

Definition 4.1 (Viscosity Solution). A function \(u \in C(\mathbb{R}^n \times [0,T))\) is called:

  1. A viscosity subsolution of \(u_t + H(x,t,u,Du) = 0\) if for every \(\phi \in C^1(\mathbb{R}^n \times [0,T))\) and every local maximum point \((x_0,t_0)\) of \(u - \phi\), we have: \[\phi_t(x_0,t_0) + H(x_0,t_0,u(x_0,t_0),D\phi(x_0,t_0)) \leq 0\]

  2. A viscosity supersolution if for every \(\phi \in C^1(\mathbb{R}^n \times [0,T))\) and every local minimum point \((x_0,t_0)\) of \(u - \phi\), we have: \[\phi_t(x_0,t_0) + H(x_0,t_0,u(x_0,t_0),D\phi(x_0,t_0)) \geq 0\]

  3. A viscosity solution if it is both a viscosity subsolution and supersolution.

The key insight is that viscosity solutions test the equation only at points where the function can be “touched” by smooth test functions, avoiding the need for derivatives to exist everywhere.

4.3.1 4.3.1 Equivalent Characterizations

For convex Hamiltonians, viscosity solutions admit several equivalent characterizations:

Theorem 4.3 (Semi-Differential Characterization). Assume \(H(x,t,u,p)\) is convex in \(p\). Then \(u\) is a viscosity solution if and only if:

  1. At every point \((x_0,t_0)\) where \(u\) is differentiable: \[u_t(x_0,t_0) + H(x_0,t_0,u(x_0,t_0),Du(x_0,t_0)) = 0\]

  2. At every point \((x_0,t_0)\) where \(u\) is not differentiable: \[\max_{p \in \partial^- u(x_0,t_0)} \{u_t^-(x_0,t_0) + H(x_0,t_0,u(x_0,t_0),p)\} \leq 0\] \[\min_{p \in \partial^+ u(x_0,t_0)} \{u_t^+(x_0,t_0) + H(x_0,t_0,u(x_0,t_0),p)\} \geq 0\]

where \(\partial^-u\) and \(\partial^+u\) denote the sub- and super-differentials of \(u\).

4.3.2 4.3.2 Stability and Consistency

A crucial property of viscosity solutions is their stability under uniform convergence:

Theorem 4.4 (Stability). Let \(\{u_\epsilon\}\) be a sequence of viscosity solutions to \(u_t + H_\epsilon(x,t,u,Du) = 0\) converging uniformly to \(u\) on compact sets. If \(H_\epsilon \to H\) uniformly on compact sets, then \(u\) is a viscosity solution of \(u_t + H(x,t,u,Du) = 0\).

This stability property makes viscosity solutions the natural solution concept for numerical approximations and homogenization problems.

4.4 4.4 Uniqueness and the Comparison Principle

The fundamental theoretical result for viscosity solutions is the comparison principle, which immediately implies uniqueness.

Theorem 4.5 (Comparison Principle). Assume \(H(x,t,u,p)\) satisfies:

  1. Continuity: \(H\) is continuous in all variables
  2. Monotonicity: \(H(x,t,u,p) \leq H(x,t,v,p)\) for \(u \geq v\)
  3. Coercivity: \(\lim_{|p| \to \infty} H(x,t,u,p) = +\infty\) uniformly in \((x,t,u)\)

Let \(u\) be a viscosity subsolution and \(v\) be a viscosity supersolution of \(u_t + H(x,t,u,Du) = 0\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n \times (0,T)\) with \(u(x,0) \leq v(x,0)\) for all \(x\). Then \(u(x,t) \leq v(x,t)\) for all \((x,t) \in \mathbb{R}^n \times [0,T)\).

Proof Sketch. The proof uses the doubling of variables technique. Assume for contradiction that \(\max_{[0,T]} \sup_{\mathbb{R}^n} (u - v) > 0\). Define the penalized function:

\[\Phi_\epsilon(x,y,t) = u(x,t) - v(y,t) - \frac{|x-y|^2}{2\epsilon}\]

The maximum of \(\Phi_\epsilon\) is achieved at some point \((x_\epsilon, y_\epsilon, t_\epsilon)\). As \(\epsilon \to 0\), we have \(x_\epsilon \to y_\epsilon\) and can construct appropriate test functions to derive a contradiction using the viscosity inequalities. □

Corollary 4.6 (Uniqueness). Under the assumptions of Theorem 4.5, the viscosity solution is unique.

4.5 4.5 Existence via Perron’s Method

Existence of viscosity solutions can be established through various approaches. We present Perron’s method, which constructs the solution as an envelope of subsolutions.

Definition 4.2 (Perron Envelope). Given a family \(\mathcal{S}\) of viscosity subsolutions, define the upper envelope: \[u(x,t) = \sup\{w(x,t) : w \in \mathcal{S}\}\]

Theorem 4.7 (Perron’s Method). Assume the Hamiltonian \(H\) satisfies the conditions of Theorem 4.5. Let \(\mathcal{S}\) be the family of all viscosity subsolutions \(w\) with \(w(x,0) \leq u_0(x)\). If the upper envelope \(u\) is continuous, then \(u\) is the unique viscosity solution.

The key technical challenge is proving continuity of the envelope. This typically requires constructing barrier functions and using the comparison principle.

using ForwardDiff

function viscosity_test_function_approach(x, t, u_func, test_phi, epsilon=1e-6)
    """
    Test if u satisfies viscosity subsolution property at (x,t)
    for Hamilton-Jacobi equation u_t + (1/2)|∇u|^2 = 0
    """
    
    # Evaluate functions and derivatives of test function
    phi_val = test_phi(x, t)
    phi_t = ForwardDiff.derivative(s -> test_phi(x, s), t)
    phi_x = ForwardDiff.gradient(y -> test_phi(y, t), x)
    
    # Check if (x,t) is a local maximum of u - phi
    is_maximum = true
    for i in 1:5  # Check neighborhood
        for j in 1:5
            x_test = x + (i-3) * epsilon
            t_test = t + (j-3) * epsilon
            
            if t_test ≥ 0
                diff_val = (u_func(x_test, t_test) - test_phi(x_test, t_test))
                if diff_val > u_func(x, t) - phi_val + epsilon^2
                    is_maximum = false
                    break
                end
            end
        end
        if !is_maximum break end
    end
    
    if is_maximum
        # Test viscosity inequality: φ_t + H(x,t,u,∇φ) ≤ 0
        H_val = 0.5 * dot(phi_x, phi_x)  # Hamiltonian |p|^2/2
        return phi_t + H_val ≤ epsilon, phi_t + H_val
    else
        return true, 0.0  # Not a test point
    end
end

# Example: Test the classical solution u(x,t) = x²/(2(1+t))
function classical_solution(x, t)
    if t > -1
        return x^2 / (2 * (1 + t))
    else
        return Inf
    end
end

# Test with a quadratic test function
test_phi(x, t) = (x^2 + 0.1) / (2 * (1 + t + 0.01))

# Test at various points
test_points = [(0.0, 0.1), (1.0, 0.5), (2.0, 0.2)]
for (x, t) in test_points
    is_valid, residual = viscosity_test_function_approach(
        [x], t, (x_vec, t_val) -> classical_solution(x_vec[1], t_val), 
        (x_vec, t_val) -> test_phi(x_vec[1], t_val)
    )
    println("Point ($x, $t): Valid = $is_valid, Residual = $residual")
end

4.6 4.6 Level Set Methods and Interface Propagation

Level set methods, introduced by Osher and Sethian, use Hamilton-Jacobi equations to track moving interfaces and geometric fronts. The key idea is to represent a moving interface \(\Gamma(t)\) as the zero level set of a function \(\phi(x,t)\).

4.6.1 4.6.1 Level Set Formulation

Consider an interface \(\Gamma(t)\) moving with normal velocity \(V\). The level set function \(\phi(x,t)\) evolves according to:

\[\phi_t + V|\nabla \phi| = 0\]

The interface is recovered as \(\Gamma(t) = \{x : \phi(x,t) = 0\}\). The normal velocity \(V\) may depend on curvature, position, or other geometric quantities.

Example 4.3 (Mean Curvature Flow). For mean curvature flow, the normal velocity is \(V = -\kappa\) where \(\kappa\) is the mean curvature. The level set equation becomes:

\[\phi_t = \kappa |\nabla \phi| = \nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\nabla \phi}{|\nabla \phi|} \right) |\nabla \phi|\]

This is a geometric parabolic equation that can be solved using standard numerical methods.

4.6.2 4.6.2 Reinitialization and Distance Functions

A practical challenge in level set methods is maintaining \(\phi\) as a distance function (\(|\nabla \phi| = 1\)). This is achieved through reinitialization, solving:

\[\psi_\tau + \text{sign}(\phi)(|\nabla \psi| - 1) = 0\]

where \(\tau\) is artificial time and \(\psi(\cdot, 0) = \phi(\cdot, t_n)\).

4.6.3 4.6.3 Fast Marching Method

For static Hamilton-Jacobi equations \(H(x, \nabla u) = 0\) with \(u = 0\) on \(\Gamma\), the fast marching method provides an efficient \(O(N \log N)\) algorithm to compute the distance function.

using DataStructures, LinearAlgebra

struct FastMarchingGrid
    nx::Int
    ny::Int
    dx::Float64
    dy::Float64
    u::Matrix{Float64}
    status::Matrix{Int}  # 0: far, 1: narrow band, 2: frozen
    heap::PriorityQueue{Tuple{Int,Int}, Float64}
end

function initialize_fast_marching(nx, ny, dx, dy, boundary_points)
    """
    Initialize fast marching method for computing distance function
    """
    u = fill(Inf, nx, ny)
    status = zeros(Int, nx, ny)
    heap = PriorityQueue{Tuple{Int,Int}, Float64}()
    
    # Set boundary conditions
    for (i, j) in boundary_points
        u[i, j] = 0.0
        status[i, j] = 2  # frozen
        
        # Add neighbors to narrow band
        for (di, dj) in [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)]
            ni, nj = i + di, j + dj
            if 1 ≤ ni ≤ nx && 1 ≤ nj ≤ ny && status[ni, nj] == 0
                status[ni, nj] = 1
                u[ni, nj] = compute_distance_estimate(u, ni, nj, dx, dy)
                heap[(ni, nj)] = u[ni, nj]
            end
        end
    end
    
    return FastMarchingGrid(nx, ny, dx, dy, u, status, heap)
end

function compute_distance_estimate(u, i, j, dx, dy)
    """
    Solve the upwind finite difference equation for |∇u| = 1
    """
    # Get upwind differences
    ux_minus = i > 1 ? (u[i,j] - u[i-1,j]) / dx : Inf
    ux_plus = i < size(u,1) ? (u[i+1,j] - u[i,j]) / dx : Inf
    uy_minus = j > 1 ? (u[i,j] - u[i,j-1]) / dy : Inf
    uy_plus = j < size(u,2) ? (u[i,j+1] - u[i,j]) / dy : Inf
    
    # Take minimum (upwind) values
    ux = min(ux_minus, ux_plus)
    uy = min(uy_minus, uy_plus)
    
    # Solve quadratic equation from upwind scheme
    if ux == Inf && uy == Inf
        return Inf
    elseif ux == Inf
        return minimum([u[i,j-1] + dy, u[i,j+1] + dy])
    elseif uy == Inf
        return minimum([u[i-1,j] + dx, u[i+1,j] + dx])
    else
        # Solve (u - ux*dx)²/dx² + (u - uy*dy)²/dy² = 1
        a = 1/dx^2 + 1/dy^2
        b = -2*(ux/dx + uy/dy)
        c = ux^2 + uy^2 - 1
        
        discriminant = b^2 - 4*a*c
        if discriminant ≥ 0
            return (-b + sqrt(discriminant)) / (2*a)
        else
            return min(ux + dx, uy + dy)
        end
    end
end

function fast_marching_step!(grid::FastMarchingGrid)
    """
    Perform one step of fast marching algorithm
    """
    if isempty(grid.heap)
        return false
    end
    
    # Extract minimum
    (i, j), u_val = dequeue_pair!(grid.heap)
    grid.status[i, j] = 2  # frozen
    
    # Update neighbors
    for (di, dj) in [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)]
        ni, nj = i + di, j + dj
        
        if 1 ≤ ni ≤ grid.nx && 1 ≤ nj ≤ grid.ny && grid.status[ni, nj] != 2
            new_u = compute_distance_estimate(grid.u, ni, nj, grid.dx, grid.dy)
            
            if new_u < grid.u[ni, nj]
                grid.u[ni, nj] = new_u
                
                if grid.status[ni, nj] == 0  # far point
                    grid.status[ni, nj] = 1
                    grid.heap[(ni, nj)] = new_u
                else  # already in narrow band, update priority
                    grid.heap[(ni, nj)] = new_u
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    return true
end

# Example: Compute distance from a circle
function example_distance_computation()
    nx, ny = 101, 101
    dx, dy = 0.1, 0.1
    
    # Define circular boundary
    center_x, center_y = 51, 51
    radius = 20
    boundary_points = Tuple{Int,Int}[]
    
    for i in 1:nx, j in 1:ny
        dist_to_center = sqrt((i - center_x)^2 + (j - center_y)^2)
        if abs(dist_to_center - radius) < 1.5
